name: I.B.E.W. - Local No. 5
description_short: Federal credit union serving I.B.E.W. Local No. 5 members in Pittsburgh with $13.9M in assets, 1,541 members, and one branch location since 1974.
description_long:
I.B.E.W. - Local No. 5 Federal Credit Union is a member-owned financial institution headquartered in Pittsburgh, PA that has operated for 52 years since its founding in 1974. It serves members affiliated with the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers Local No. 5, functioning as an associational credit union. The institution maintains one branch office at 5 Hot Metal Street in Pittsburgh's Strip District and is federally chartered under charter number 21782.

The credit union offers core banking services including checking and savings accounts, ATM/debit card access, and various loan products. Based on their financial statements, they provide real estate loans, unsecured personal loans, and other loan categories. As of September 30, 2025, they reported $13.93 million in total assets with $11.73 million in member shares and deposits, indicating a conservative and stable financial position. Their loan portfolio totals $7.6 million across multiple categories.

As a specialized associational credit union, I.B.E.W. - Local No. 5 distinguishes itself by serving a specific occupational community of electrical workers rather than the general public. This membership focus allows them to tailor products and services to the unique financial needs of union members. The organization is member-governed with Michael J. Berta serving as Chairperson and Kathleen Saffer as CEO/Manager. Their most recent audit was completed on November 1, 2024.

This is a small, stable community credit union best suited for members of I.B.E.W. Local No. 5 seeking basic banking services and member-focused lending. The institution's modest asset base and single branch location mean limited convenience compared to larger banks, and service capabilities are constrained by their small operational scale. However, they demonstrate financial stability with positive net income of $217,153 and a healthy equity-to-asset ratio of 15.5%.
pros: Member-owned structure providing governance and profit-sharing benefits for I.B.E.W. Local No. 5 members
52-year operating history demonstrating institutional stability and longevity
Positive net income of $217,153 and healthy equity position of $2.16 million
Federally insured deposits through NCUSIF with lower regulatory risk
Tailored services designed specifically for electrical workers and union members
Personal relationship banking approach typical of credit union operations
Loan portfolio spanning real estate, unsecured, and other categories totaling $7.6 million
cons: Single branch location limits physical accessibility and convenience compared to regional or national banks
Small asset base of $13.9 million constrains lending capacity and product innovation compared to larger institutions
Limited membership base of 1,541 members restricts eligibility to I.B.E.W. Local No. 5 affiliates only
No FHLB membership may limit access to wholesale funding sources and emergency liquidity
Small operations may result in limited customer service hours and fewer digital banking amenities
best_for: Current and retired I.B.E.W. Local No. 5 electrical workers seeking member-focused banking
Union members wanting to keep banking relationships within their occupational community
Individuals seeking basic checking, savings, and small loan products without complexity
diagnosis: I.B.E.W. - Local No. 5 Federal Credit Union is best for members of the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers Local No. 5 seeking straightforward banking and lending services from a member-owned institution. The main caveat is that membership is restricted to union-affiliated individuals, and the single-branch, small-asset operation provides limited convenience and product breadth compared to larger financial institutions.
typical_results_timeline: Membership approval and account opening typically takes 1-3 business days. Loan decisions are usually faster than traditional banks.
